args.thought:
The user is absolutely right. IPFS stores blocks as files on disk. We own the filesystem. We can just write the blocks directly to the IPFS block store directory, which is typically at ~/.ipfs/blocks/. Each block is stored as a file named by its multihash. If we compute the CID correctly and write the block data to the right path, `ipfs cat` will find it.

Let me check where the IPFS repo is and how blocks are stored.

The IPFS block store structure:
- ~/.ipfs/blocks/SHARDING_PREFIX/MULTIHASH.data
- The sharding is based on the second-to-last two characters of the base32 CID

Actually, IPFS uses a flatfs datastore. Blocks are stored at:
~/.ipfs/blocks/XX/CIDBASE32.data

Where XX is derived from the CID. Let me check the actual structure.

results.title: Remove cross-codec conversion from HTTP Gateways
results.snippet:
When the block's CID codec matches the requested response format,
implementations MAY return the block as-is without parsing or validating it.

results.title: UnixFS CID Profiles
results.snippet: While CIDs and UnixFS DAGs are cryptographically verifiable, the same file or directory can produce different CIDs across UnixFS implementations, because DAG construction parameters like chunk size, DAG width, and layout vary between tools. Often, these parameters are not even configurable by users.

results.title: rust-unixfs — Rust filesystem library // Lib.rs
results.snippet: The main entry point for extracting information and/or data out of UnixFs trees is ipfs_unixfs :: walk :: Walker. To resolve IpfsPath segments over dag-pb nodes, ipfs_unixfs :: resolve should be used.

results.title: IPFS data types — ipfs-search  documentation
results.snippet: Content identifiers CID in IPFS currently exist in two different versions: CIDv0 and CIDv1. Version 1 is self-descriptive in that the base, codec (format), CID version and hash are contained within the identifier. In version 0 these predefined in the format.

results.title: Are Peer IDs valid CID?
results.snippet: There are two ways to represent peer IDs in text: as a raw base58btc encoded multihash (e.g., Qm..., 1... ) and as a multibase encoded CID (e.g., bafz... ).

results.title: IPLD ♦ DAG-PB Specification
results.snippet: DAG-PB is an IPLD codec that uses Protocol Buffers to describe a binary format that can encode a byte array and an associated list of links. It is the primary means of encoding structured file data for IPFS, serving as the encoded data carrier for UnixFS.

results.title: What codec use?
results.snippet: The RAW codec is only used for leaves. If you had a folder, or a file larger than the chunking size, you would get CIDv1s that use the dab-pb codec instead. In the case of raw leaves there is simply nothing to interpret on how to parse the content that is referenced by the CID, as it is just raw data.

A small, fast and reliable ipfs implementation designed for embedding in to complex p2p
applications.
node discovery via mdns provider discovery via kademlia exchange blocks via bitswap lru eviction policy aliases, an abstraction of recursively named pins temporary recursive pins for building dags, preventing races with the garbage collector efficiently syncing large dags of blocks

use ipfs_embed :: { Config, DefaultParams, Ipfs } ; use libipld :: DagCbor ; use libipld :: store :: Store ; # [ derive ( Clone, DagCbor, Debug, Eq, PartialEq ) ] struct Identity { id : u64, name : String, age : u8, } # [ async_std :: main ] async fn main ( ) -> Result < ( ), Box < dyn std :: error :: Error > > { let cache_size = 10 ; let ipfs = Ipfs :: < DefaultParams > :: new ( Config :: new ( None, cache_size ) ).

results.title: ipfs-sqlite-block-store — Rust caching library // Lib.rs
results.snippet:
Sqlite based block store for content-addressed data like IPLD.
See the module level docs for more details.
lib.rs :
A block store for a rust implementation of ipfs.
Aliases
An alias is a named pin of a root. When a root is aliased, none of the leaves of the dag pointed
to by the root will be collected by gc. However, a root being aliased does not mean that the dag
must be complete.
Temporary aliases
A temporary alias is an unnamed alias that is just for the purpose of protecting blocks from gc
while a large tree is being constructed. While an alias maps a single name to a single root, a
temporary alias can be assigned to an arbitary number of blocks before the dag is finished.
A temporary alias will be deleted as soon as the handle goes out of scope.
Garbage Collection (GC)
GC refers to the process of removing unpinned blocks. It runs only when the configured size
targets are exceeded. Size targets contain both the total size of the store
and the number of blocks.
GC will run incrementally, deleting blocks until the size targets are no longer exceeded. The
order in which unpinned blocks will be deleted can be customized.
For unpinned blocks, it is possible to customize which blocks have the highest value using a CacheTracker. The default is to do nothing and has no performance overhead.

results.title: multiformats
results.snippet: This library defines common interfaces and low level building blocks for various interrelated multiformat technologies (multicodec, multihash, multibase, and CID). They can be used to implement custom base encoders / decoders / codecs, codec encoders /decoders and multihash hashers that comply to the interface that layers above assume.

results.title: IPFS data types — ipfs-search  documentation
results.snippet: Content identifiers CID in IPFS currently exist in two different versions: CIDv0 and CIDv1. Version 1 is self-descriptive in that the base, codec (format), CID version and hash are contained within the identifier.

results.title: Are Peer IDs valid CID?
results.snippet: There are two ways to represent peer IDs in text: as a raw base58btc encoded multihash (e.g., Qm..., 1... ) and as a multibase encoded CID (e.g., bafz... ).

results.title: multihash
results.snippet: The multihash crate exposes a basic data structure for encoding and decoding multihash.

results.title: Anatomy of a CID (Lesson 3)
results.snippet: CIDv0 uses multihash to support multiple hashing functions. This means that we can successfully generate a hash for specific content using different hashing algorithms and later on be able to identify content using this hash.

results.title: Revision c5efb86a-abd2-4e9a-9c1f-db621f5d6358
results.snippet: First the thing you seems to have caught on but adding it for future readers, unixfs is a *merkle* datastructure, that means we use hashes as pointer, CIDs are hashes + metadata pointing to the next block.

results.title: Limit Identity CID Size to 128 Bytes in UnixFS Contexts
results.snippet: This IPIP establishes a 128-byte maximum digest size limit for identity CIDs (multihash code 0x00 ) in UnixFS contexts to prevent abuse and clarify appropriate usage boundaries.

results.title: Confusion betweed CID and "bare"/"naked"/"raw" Multihash
results.snippet:
(Here, if the hash is sha256, and the length is 32, it almost a CIDv0. We just have to encode it in base58btc, right?)
Since we want a CIDv1:
Concat( multicodec_code_for_multihashes, multihash) = a multicodec (specifically a multihash's multicodec, as a binary)
(and the multicodec_code_for_multihashes = 0x31)
Concat ("0x01",multicodec) = something almost useful (as binary)
(and “0x01” is the version of the CID)
Encode(previous binary) = a string of characters
Concat(code_for_this_encoding, previous string) = a multibase, and more specificaly a CIDv1 (as a string)

ipfs-unixfs: UnixFs tree support in Rust. The crate aims to provide a blockstore implementation independent of the UnixFs implementation by working on slices and not doing any IO operations. The main entry point for extracting information and/or data out of UnixFs trees is `ipfs_unixfs::walk::Walker`. To resolve `IpfsPath` segments over dag-pb nodes, `ipfs_unixfs::resolve` should be used. # ipfs-unixfs

## Goals
* blockstore API independent way to traverse the merkledag
  * the core read API does not deal with loading blocks
  * instead access to interesting `Cid`s is given

## Status
* unfiltered walking of known unixfs trees
* creation of balanced file trees
* creation of non HAMT-sharded directory trees

See the docs at [https://docs. rs/ipfs-unixfs][19]. ## License

MIT or APL2. #### Dependencies

ipfs-unixfs: UnixFs tree support in Rust. The crate aims to provide a blockstore implementation independent of the UnixFs implementation by working on slices and not doing any IO operations. The main entry point for extracting information and/or data out of UnixFs trees is `ipfs_unixfs::walk::Walker`. *`pub use dir::[ResolveError][60];`*

## Modules[§][61]

*[dagpb][62]*
  Support operations for the dag-pb, the outer shell of UnixFS
*[dir][63]*
  Directory and directory tree support
*[file][64]*
  File support. *[symlink][65]*
  Symlink creation support UnixFS symlink support. UnixFS symlinks are UnixFS messages similar to single block files, but the link name or target path is encoded in the UnixFS::Data field. This means that the target path could be in any encoding, however it is always treated as an utf8 Unix path. Could be that this is wrong. *[walk][66]*
  Support for walking over all UnixFs trees

## Structs[§][67]

*[InvalidCidInLink][68]*
  A link could not be transformed into a Cid. *[Metadata][69]*
  A container for the UnixFs metadata, which can be present at the root of the file, directory, or symlink trees. *[UnexpectedNodeType][70]*
  Wrapper around the unexpected UnixFs node type, allowing access to querying what is known about the type. [1]: /

rust-ipfs / rust_ipfs: This project aims to be a full, library-first, production-ready implementation of an IPFS node written in native Rust.
It is built on top of Parity's foundational work in rust-libp2p, which is already used by other major projects like Polkadot.
It can be used as an embedded library within other Rust applications, suitable for high-performance or resource-constrained environments.
ipfs-embed: This is a separate, small, embeddable IPFS implementation, focusing on core functionalities and designed for integration into applications where a lightweight footprint is important.
ipfs-api: For use cases where running a separate, external IPFS node (like the reference Go-IPFS implementation) is acceptable, the ipfs-api crate provides a client library to interact with the HTTP API of that running node. This requires a running IPFS daemon.

Now I see the layout. rust-ipfs uses `second-to-last/2` sharding with CIDv1 string as filename — same as go-ipfs. But this is a different sharding than what's in `~/.ipfs/blocks/` (which uses base32upper multihash keys). Let me check:

args.thought:
So go-ipfs uses base32upper multihash keys (CIQ... prefix = base32upper of the raw multihash), while rust-ipfs uses CIDv1 base32lower strings (bafyb...) as filenames. They're different formats for the same sharding scheme (next-to-last/2).

The user wants to write blocks that `ipfs cat` (go-ipfs/kubo) can read. So we need to use the go-ipfs format: base32upper multihash keys.

But actually, the user said "use a standard rust lib for this, we don't need to reinvent it". They want us to use the rust-ipfs submodule. 

The simplest approach: use rust-ipfs's FsBlockStore but point it at ~/.ipfs/blocks/. But the key format is different from go-ipfs...

Wait, let me re-read. The rust-ipfs FsBlockStore uses CIDv1 string as the filename (e.g., `bafybeiciz...`), while go-ipfs uses base32upper multihash (e.g., `CIQIWIBZ...`). They're incompatible.

But the user said "we own the filesystem, we can write the ipfs files". Maybe we should just use the rust-ipfs FsBlockStore with its own block directory, and then also have a way to read from it. Or we could adapt it to write in go-ipfs format.

Actually, the simplest thing: use rust-ipfs's unixfs crate to properly encode the block (dag-pb + unixfs protobuf), then use the `cid` crate to compute the proper CID, and write the block to go-ipfs's flatfs format. We don't need the full rust-ipfs node, just the encoding + file writing.

Let me look at what the unixfs crate provides for creating files/blocks.

ToolUse.content: Now I have the full picture. `FileAdder` produces `(Cid, Vec<u8>)` blocks. We just need to write those blocks to `~/.ipfs/blocks/` in go-ipfs format. Let me check the go-ipfs key format — it uses base32upper of the raw multihash:

args.command:
# Decode a go-ipfs block filename to understand the format
# CIQIWIBZMYOEGRMJRJSVY7QXIJO662TNLP7QW2MPEN32XKRX33SUZUY
# CIQ prefix = base32upper of multihash starting with 0x12 0x20 (sha256)
python3 -c "
import base64
key = 'CIQIWIBZMYOEGRMJRJSVY7QXIJO662TNLP7QW2MPEN32XKRX33SUZUY'
# base32upper decode
raw = base64.b32decode(key + '=' * (-len(key) % 8))
print('raw bytes:', raw[:4].hex(), '...')
print('first byte (hash fn):', hex(raw[0]))
print('second byte (digest len):', raw[1])
"

ToolUse.content: Confirmed — go-ipfs block keys are base32upper of the raw multihash. Now let me check what CID version `FileAdder` produces:

It produces CIDv0. For go-ipfs compatibility with `ipfs cat`, that's fine — go-ipfs can read both v0 and v1. The block key in flatfs is the base32upper multihash regardless.

Now let me wire this up. We'll use `FileAdder` from the submodule to produce proper blocks, then write them to `~/.ipfs/blocks/` in go-ipfs format:
Now update our Cargo.toml to use the submodule's unixfs crate as a path dependency:

ToolUse.content: Now rewrite `ipfs.rs` to use `FileAdder` and write blocks to go-ipfs flatfs:

args.thought:
Now I need to replace the hand-rolled UnixFS/dag-pb encoding with the proper `rust-unixfs` FileAdder, and write blocks to go-ipfs flatfs format.

The key insight:
1. `FileAdder` produces `(Cid, Vec<u8>)` pairs — CIDv0 + raw block bytes
2. Go-ipfs flatfs stores blocks as: `~/.ipfs/blocks/XX/KEY.data` where KEY is base32upper of the multihash, and XX is next-to-last/2 chars of KEY
3. The final CID (root) is what we return — can be CIDv0 (Qm...) or converted to CIDv1

Let me rewrite ipfs.rs to:
- Use FileAdder for proper block encoding
- Write each block to ~/.ipfs/blocks/ in go-ipfs format
- Return the root CID (CIDv0 for go-ipfs compat, or convert to CIDv1)
- Keep the DASL/CBOR wrapping
- Keep the ContentStore trait

args.thought:
The problem is that `builtins.path` in nix still uses git to determine what to copy when inside a flake. In a flake context, nix always uses the git tree as the source, even with `builtins.path`. Submodules are stored as gitlinks in the parent repo, so the actual content isn't part of the parent git tree.

Options:
1. Use `fetchGit` with `submodules = true` outside of pure eval mode
2. Copy just the unixfs crate directly into the repo (not as a submodule) 
3. Use the crate from crates.io instead of the submodule
4. Use a `filterSource` that actually copies the filesystem

Actually, the simplest approach that respects the user's wish to keep the submodule: use `rust-unixfs` from crates.io as a regular dependency in Cargo.toml, and keep the submodule for reference/development. The crate is published on crates.io as version 0.5.0.

Or even simpler: just copy the unixfs directory out of the submodule into vendor/rust-unixfs/ as a regular directory (not a submodule). The submodule stays for the full rust-ipfs reference.

Actually, the correct way in flakes is to override the self input. But you can't do that directly. The standard approach is:

```nix
inputs.self.url = "git+file:.?submodules=1";
```

No wait, that's not valid either. Let me think...

The proper way: in the flake, you can't easily get submodules from `self`. The recommended approaches are:
1. Add the submodule repo as a separate flake input
2. Use `builtins.fetchGit` with `submodules = true` (but this fails in pure eval)
3. Use `--impure` flag
4. Copy the needed files directly

args.new_str:
src = pkgs.runCommand "source-with-submodules" {} ''
              cp -r ${self} $out
              chmod -R u+w $out
              mkdir -p $out/vendor
              cp -r ${rust-ipfs} $out/vendor/rust-ipfs
            '';
args.old_str: src = builtins.path { path = ./.; name = "source"; filter = path: type: true; };

args.file_text:
# IPFS Integration — Pure Rust, No Daemon

kant-pastebin computes IPFS content identifiers and writes blocks directly
to the local go-ipfs/kubo block store. No daemon process, no HTTP API, no
CLI shelling — just filesystem writes.

## Architecture

```
content bytes
      │
      ▼
┌─────────────┐
│  FileAdder  │  rust-unixfs crate (git submodule: vendor/rust-ipfs)
│  (UnixFS    │  Encodes content as dag-pb protobuf blocks.
│   dag-pb)   │  Chunks large files into a balanced Merkle DAG.
└──────┬──────┘
       │ yields (CID, block_bytes) pairs
       ▼
┌─────────────┐
│ write_block │  Writes each block to go-ipfs flatfs:
│  (flatfs)   │    ~/.ipfs/blocks/{shard}/{key}.data
└──────┬──────┘
       │ returns root CID
       ▼
┌─────────────┐
│ paste header│  Stored as IPFS: QmcFFn... in paste file
│  + JSON API │  Returned as ipfs_cid in POST /paste response
└─────────────┘
```

## Flatfs Layout

go-ipfs stores blocks in a sharded directory tree under `~/.ipfs/blocks/`.
The sharding scheme is **next-to-last/2**:

```
key   = BASE32_NOPAD_UPPER(multihash)
shard = key[len-3 .. len-1]   (2nd-to-last two characters)
path  = ~/.ipfs/blocks/{shard}/{key}.data
```

Example for `QmcFFnmxaUcMiF7ePCtqndf7rfZzsG6jrW46gDM7ToD2q9`:

```
multihash = 1220... (0x12=sha256, 0x20=32 bytes, then digest)
key       = CIQO...XY
shard     = last-2-before-last char of key
path      = ~/.ipfs/blocks/XY/CIQO...XY.data
```

## CID Versions

- **CIDv0** (`Qm...`): Returned by `FileAdder` (base58btc, dag-pb + sha256).
  This is what `ipfs add` produces by default.
- **CIDv1** (`bafy...`): Available via `cid_to_v1()` for display.
  Same content, different encoding.

Both versions resolve to the same multihash and the same flatfs block path.

## Data Flow on Paste Creation

```
POST /paste {"content": "hello"}
  → handlers::create_paste()
    → ipfs::ipfs_add("hello")
      → FileAdder::push(b"hello")
      → FileAdder::finish()
        → yields (Qm..., <dag-pb block bytes>)
      → write_block() → ~/.ipfs/blocks/XX/KEY.data
    → dasl::dasl_cid(b"hello")  → 0xda51...
    → write paste file with IPFS: and DASL: headers
  ← {"ipfs_cid": "Qm...", ...}
```

## Verification

```sh
# Create a paste
curl -s -X POST http://127.0.0.1:8090/paste \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"content":"test"}' | jq .ipfs_cid

# Read it back via ipfs (no daemon needed, reads flatfs directly)
ipfs cat QmcFFnmxaUcMiF7ePCtqndf7rfZzsG6jrW46gDM7ToD2q9
```

## ContentStore Backends

| Backend        | How it works                              | Use case          |
|----------------|-------------------------------------------|-------------------|
| `RustStore`    | `rust-unixfs` FileAdder + flatfs write    | Default, WASM-ok  |
| `DaslCborStore`| CBOR envelope → then RustStore            | Monster symmetry  |
| `IpfsCliStore` | Shells out to `ipfs add`                  | Fallback          |

## DASL/CBOR Envelope

`wrap_dasl_cbor()` creates a CBOR object containing:

| Field       | Description                                    |
|-------------|------------------------------------------------|
| `prefix`    | `0xDA51` marker                                |
| `dasl_type` | 3 (nested CID)                                 |
| `orbifold`  | (l mod 71, m mod 59, n mod 47) coordinates     |
| `bott`      | Bott periodicity index (0–7)                   |
| `data`      | Raw content bytes                              |
| `cid`       | IPFS CID string                                |
| `dasl`      | 64-bit DASL Monster symmetry address           |

## Dependencies

- `rust-unixfs` — UnixFS dag-pb encoding (git submodule at `vendor/rust-ipfs`)
- `ipld-core` — CID type
- `data-encoding` — base32 encoding for flatfs keys
- `dirs-next` — home directory resolution
- `sha2` — SHA-256 for local CID and DASL
- `ciborium` — CBOR serialization for DASL envelopes

## Repo Path Resolution

1. `$IPFS_PATH` environment variable (if set)
2. `~/.ipfs` (default)
3. If neither exists, blocks are not written (CID still computed)

Json.stdout:
# kant-pastebin

Kant Pastebin - UUCP + zkTLS + IPFS

## Quick Info

- **Port**: 8080
- **Source**: ~/git/kant-pastebin
- **Service**: systemctl --user status kant-pastebin.service

## Features

### IPFS Integration (Added 2026-03-08)

The pastebin now supports Content-Addressed Storage via IPFS:

- **CID/IPFS Addressing**: Paste endpoint accepts filename, local CID, or IPFS CID
- **Lookup Priority**: filename → .cid mapping → IPFS fetch
- **P2P Content Retrieval**: Fetch content directly from IPFS network using CID
- **UTF-8 Emoji Rendering**: Proper HTML encoding with monospace styling
- **Content Verification**: SHA256 witness hashing for integrity

#### Usage

Post content with IPFS CID:
```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/paste \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"cid": "QmYourIPFSCID", "title": "My Paste"}'
```

58 /mnt/data1/kant/pastebin/DOCS.md
# kant-pastebin

Kant Pastebin - UUCP + zkTLS + IPFS

## Quick Info

- **Port**: 8080
- **Source**: ~/git/kant-pastebin
- **Service**: systemctl --user status kant-pastebin.service

## Features

### IPFS Integration (Added 2026-03-08)

The pastebin now supports Content-Addressed Storage via IPFS:

- **CID/IPFS Addressing**: Paste endpoint accepts filename, local CID, or IPFS CID
- **Lookup Priority**: filename → .cid mapping → IPFS fetch
- **P2P Content Retrieval**: Fetch content directly from IPFS network using CID
- **UTF-8 Emoji Rendering**: Proper HTML encoding with monospace styling
- **Content Verification**: SHA256 witness hashing for integrity

#### Usage

Post content with IPFS CID:
```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/paste \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"cid": "QmYourIPFSCID", "title": "My Paste"}'
```

Post content directly (generates CID):
```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/paste \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"content": "Hello World", "title": "My Paste"}'
```

#### IPFS Indexing

Add existing pastes to IPFS:
```bash
./add-to-ipfs.sh
```

## Links

- [Architecture](ARCHITECTURE.md)
- [API Documentation](API.md)
- [Diagrams](diagrams/)

## Status

Check service status:
```bash
systemctl --user status kant-pastebin.service
curl http://localhost:8080/health
```

args.new_str:
### IPFS Integration (Updated 2026-03-14)

Pure Rust IPFS content addressing — no daemon, no CLI, no HTTP API.
Blocks are written directly to the go-ipfs/kubo flatfs block store.

- **Pure Rust encoding**: `rust-unixfs` FileAdder produces real UnixFS dag-pb blocks
- **Direct flatfs writes**: blocks go to `~/.ipfs/blocks/` (go-ipfs compatible)
- **`ipfs cat` works**: content is immediately retrievable by any IPFS tool
- **CIDv0** (`Qm...`): default, same as `ipfs add`
- **DASL/CBOR envelopes**: Monster symmetry metadata alongside IPFS CIDs
- **Three backends**: RustStore (default), DaslCborStore, IpfsCliStore (fallback)

See [IPFS.md](IPFS.md) for full architecture details.

#### Usage

```bash
# Create paste — returns IPFS CID
curl -X POST http://localhost:8090/paste \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"content": "Hello World"}'
# → {"ipfs_cid": "Qm...", "cid": "bafk...", ...}

# Verify with ipfs cat (reads flatfs directly, no daemon)
ipfs cat QmcFFnmxaUcMiF7ePCtqndf7rfZzsG6jrW46gDM7ToD2q9
```

# Escaped RDFa (eRDFa) - Complete Framework

A cryptographically secure, decentralized semantic web framework with formal verification, multi-layered access control, and schema-agnostic reasoning.

## 🚀 Live Example: SolFunMeme

**First production deployment** on Solana mainnet  
→ [docs/solfunmeme-example.md](docs/solfunmeme-example.md)

## Overview

Escaped RDFa enables embedding structured semantic data in hostile environments through steganography, cryptography, and blockchain technology, with mathematical proofs of correctness.

## Features

### 🔐 Cryptographic Security
- **Lattice-based encryption** (quantum-resistant)
- **Reed-Solomon error correction** (2^n redundancy)
- **Zero-knowledge proofs** (privacy-preserving)
- **Homomorphic encryption** (compute on encrypted data)
- **Multi-channel encoding** (8+ encoding channels)

### 🎯 Access Control
- **Multi-layered ACL** (5 access levels)
- **Nested encryption** (hierarchical access)
- **Shard-based access** (top-N coin holders)
- **Threshold signatures** (k-of-n multi-sig)

### 🌐 Semantic Web
- **Schema-agnostic** (any RDF vocabulary)
- **Ontology transformation** (MiniZinc solver)
- **Agent reasoning** (pattern discovery)
- **Emergent vocabularies** (organic evolution)

### ⛓️ Blockchain
- **Proof-of-Semantic-Work** (PoSW consensus)
- **Economic incentives** (fees + rewards)
- **SPARQL queries** (over blockchain)
- **Decentralized storage** (distributed shards)

### ✅ Formal Verification
- **Lean4 proofs** (reconstruction correctness)
- **MiniZinc constraints** (ontology transformation)
- **Mathematical guarantees** (security + semantics)

## Architecture

```
Layer 0: Mathematical Foundations (Monster Group, Gandalf Prime)
Layer 1: Cryptographic Structures (Lattice, Reed-Solomon, ZK)
Layer 2: Steganographic Systems (Multi-channel encoding)
Layer 3: Blockchain & Economics (PoSW, incentives)
Layer 4: Access Control (ACL, shards)
Layer 5: Semantic Applications (Music, academia, DAOs)
```

## Quick Start

### Installation

//! Hostile Media Embedding (HME) Steganographic System
//! 
//! Embed structured data in hostile environments that strip metadata

/// Hostility level of environment
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, PartialOrd, Ord)]
pub enum HostilityLevel {
    Friendly = 0,        // No sanitization
    Cautious = 1,        // Basic sanitization
    Restrictive = 2,     // Whitelist-based
    Aggressive = 3,      // Blacklist + Whitelist
    Paranoid = 4,        // Complete rewrite
    MaximumHostile = 5,  // Text-only
}

/// Steganographic encoding strategy
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
pub enum StegoStrategy {
    HtmlEscape,      // Standard HTML entity escaping
    CommentEmbed,    // Hide in HTML comments
    HiddenDiv,       // Display:none div
    DataAttribute,   // data-* attributes
    Whitespace,      // Extra whitespace encoding
    ZeroWidth,       // Zero-width characters
    Unicode,         // Homoglyph substitution
    MultiLayer,      // Multiple encoding layers
    Position,        // Element positioning (x,y coordinates)
    Ordering,        // DOM element order
    Color,           // RGB/HSL color values
    CssProperty,     // CSS property values
    FontSize,        // Font size variations
    Bitmap,          // Image pixel data
    QrCode,          // QR code encoding
    VisualNoise,     // Imperceptible visual variations
}

/// Steganographic encoder trait
pub trait StegoEncoder {
    fn encode(&self, data: &str, strategy: StegoStrategy) -> String;
    fn decode(&self, encoded: &str, strategy: StegoStrategy) -> Option<String>;

# SNEAKERNET — Self-Replicating Knowledge Network A system where programs, proofs, and data travel as URLs, QR codes, WAV files, and images. Each paste is a node. Combine nodes into new nodes. The network grows. The pastebin replicates itself. ## Core Idea ``` content → IPFS CID → encode as URL/QR/WAV/morse → transmit → decode → verify CID → reconstruct ``` Every piece of data has a CID. CIDs compose. A program is a DAG of CIDs. The pastebin itself is a DAG of CIDs. Transmit the root CID through any channel — the receiver reconstructs everything from the network. ## Architecture ``` ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Static Pastebin │ │ (WASM, runs in browser) │ │ │ │ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ │ │ │ RDFa/ │ │ IPFS │ │ DASL │ │ │ │ eRDFa │ │ flatfs │ │ Monster │ │ │ │ triples │ │ blocks │ │ symmetry │ │ │ └────┬─────┘ └────┬─────┘ └────┬─────┘ │ │ └──────────────┼─────────────┘ │ │ ▼ │ │ ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐ │ │ │ Encoding Layer │ │ │ │ URL · QR · WAV · Morse · Stego │ │ │ └──────────────┬──────────────────────┘ │ │ ▼ │ │ ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐ │ │ │ Transport Layer │ │ │ │ Screen · Speaker · Camera · Mic │ │ │ │ Clipboard · LocalStorage · File │ │ │ └─────────────────────────────────────┘ │ └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` ## Phases ### Phase 1: URL Encoding (data: URIs + fragment CIDs) Encode small pastes entirely in URLs. No server needed to read them. ``` data:text/html;base64,... (self-contained paste + viewer) https://solana.solfunmeme.com/pastebin/paste/ID#ipfs:QmXYZ ``` - Paste content → IPFS block → base64 → data: URI - Fragment identifier carries the CID for verification - RDFa metadata embedded in the HTML - Combine multiple URLs: each URL is a shard, reconstruct from k-of-n ### Phase 2: QR Code Transport Encode CIDs and small payloads as QR codes. - Single QR: CID reference (46 bytes for CIDv0) - Multi-QR: chunked payload with sequence numbers + Reed-Solomon - Animated QR: cycle through chunks on screen, camera captures - RDFa triple per QR: subject/predicate/object as structured data ### Phase 3: Audio Transport (WAV/Modem/Morse) Encode data as sound. BBS modem tones, morse code, FSK. - Morse: CID as dots/dashes (human-decodable) - FSK modem: ~300 baud, reliable over speaker/mic - DTMF: hex digits as phone tones - WAV file: embed data in audio, paste as attachment ### Phase 4: WASM Static Pastebin The pastebin compiles to WASM and runs entirely in the browser. - LocalStorage as block store (flatfs layout in IndexedDB) - Service Worker for offline operation - Import/export via all transport channels - Self-replicating: the WASM binary is itself a paste with a CID ### Phase 5: Visual Transport Screen capture, image steganography, video frames. - Screenshot → OCR → extract QR/text/URLs - Stego: hide data in PNG pixel LSBs (from eRDFa stego.rs) - Video: encode data as frame sequence - Screen recording: replay to reconstruct ### Phase 6: Network Composition Combine pastes into programs. Programs into systems. - DAG of CIDs = a program/proof - Root CID = the entire system - Merkle proof of inclusion for any node - DASL orbifold coordinates for navigation - RDFa triples link nodes semantically ## eRDFa Integration From `~/02-february/22/dasl/rdfa-namespace/`: | Module | Use in Sneakernet | |--------|-------------------| | `stego.rs` | Multi-channel steganographic encoding (16 strategies) | | `crypto.rs` | Reed-Solomon error correction for lossy channels | | `shards.rs` | k-of-n shard splitting (71 shards = Gandalf prime) | | `symmetry.rs` | Monster group coordinate system | | `erdfa-wasm` | Browser runtime for parsing/reconstructing | | `acl.rs` | Access control for encrypted shards | ## Data Flow: Paste → URL → QR → WAV → Paste ``` 1. Create paste → content + CID + DASL + RDFa triples 2. Encode as URL → data:text/html;base64,... (self-viewing) 3. URL → QR code → scannable from any screen 4. QR → WAV → modem tones carrying the QR data 5. WAV → decode → reconstruct QR → URL → content 6. Verify CID → SHA256 matches, content authentic 7. Store in new pastebin → the knowledge has traveled ``` ## Self-Replication The pastebin can replicate itself: ``` 1. pastebin source code → series of IPFS blocks 2. blocks → CIDs → encoded as QR sequence 3. QR sequence displayed on screen 4. new device captures QR sequence 5. reconstructs source code from blocks 6. compiles via WASM (or nix) 7. new pastebin instance running ``` ## URL Composition Algebra URLs compose like functions: ``` paste(A) + paste(B) = paste(C) where C.content = A ∘ B CID(C) = ipfs_add(A.content + B.content) DASL(C) = dasl_merge(DASL(A), DASL(B)) // XOR in orbifold space ``` A "program" is a paste whose content references other pastes by CID. Execution = recursive CID resolution + WASM evaluation. ## File Format Each paste carries RDFa metadata: ```html
...
```

(self-contained paste + viewer) https://solana.solfunmeme.com/pastebin/paste/ID#ipfs:QmXYZ ``` - Paste content → IPFS block → base64 → data: URI - Fragment identifier carries the CID for verification - RDFa metadata embedded in the HTML - Combine multiple URLs: each URL is a shard, reconstruct from k-of-n ### Phase 2: QR Code Transport Encode CIDs and small payloads as QR codes. - Single QR: CID reference (46 bytes for CIDv0) - Multi-QR: chunked payload with sequence numbers + Reed-Solomon - Animated QR: cycle through chunks on screen, camera captures - RDFa triple per QR: subject/predicate/object as structured data ### Phase 3: Audio Transport (WAV/Modem/Morse) Encode data as sound. BBS modem tones, morse code, FSK. - Morse: CID as dots/dashes (human-decodable) - FSK modem: ~300 baud, reliable over speaker/mic - DTMF: hex digits as phone tones - WAV file: embed data in audio, paste as attachment ### Phase 4: WASM Static Pastebin The pastebin compiles to WASM and runs entirely in the browser. - LocalStorage as block store (flatfs layout in IndexedDB) - Service Worker for offline operation - Import/export via all transport channels - Self-replicating: the WASM binary is itself a paste with a CID ### Phase 5: Visual Transport Screen capture, image steganography, video frames. - Screenshot → OCR → extract QR/text/URLs - Stego: hide data in PNG pixel LSBs (from eRDFa stego.rs) - Video: encode data as frame sequence - Screen recording: replay to reconstruct ### Phase 6: Network Composition Combine pastes into programs. Programs into systems. - DAG of CIDs = a program/proof - Root CID = the entire system - Merkle proof of inclusion for any node - DASL orbifold coordinates for navigation - RDFa triples link nodes semantically ## eRDFa Integration From `~/02-february/22/dasl/rdfa-namespace/`: | Module | Use in Sneakernet | |--------|-------------------| | `stego.rs` | Multi-channel steganographic encoding (16 strategies) | | `crypto.rs` | Reed-Solomon error correction for lossy channels | | `shards.rs` | k-of-n shard splitting (71 shards = Gandalf prime) | | `symmetry.rs` | Monster group coordinate system | | `erdfa-wasm` | Browser runtime for parsing/reconstructing | | `acl.rs` | Access control for encrypted shards | ## Data Flow: Paste → URL → QR → WAV → Paste ``` 1. Create paste → content + CID + DASL + RDFa triples 2. Encode as URL → data:text/html;base64,... (self-viewing) 3. URL → QR code → scannable from any screen 4. QR → WAV → modem tones carrying the QR data 5. WAV → decode → reconstruct QR → URL → content 6. Verify CID → SHA256 matches, content authentic 7. Store in new pastebin → the knowledge has traveled ``` ## Self-Replication The pastebin can replicate itself: ``` 1. pastebin source code → series of IPFS blocks 2. blocks → CIDs → encoded as QR sequence 3. QR sequence displayed on screen 4. new device captures QR sequence 5. reconstructs source code from blocks 6. compiles via WASM (or nix) 7. new pastebin instance running ``` ## URL Composition Algebra URLs compose like functions: ``` paste(A) + paste(B) = paste(C) where C.content = A ∘ B CID(C) = ipfs_add(A.content + B.content) DASL(C) = dasl_merge(DASL(A), DASL(B)) // XOR in orbifold space ``` A "program" is a paste whose content references other pastes by CID. Execution = recursive CID resolution + WASM evaluation. ## File Format Each paste carries RDFa metadata: ```html
...
```

Response.content:
Here's the vision documented in `SNEAKERNET.md`. Six phases:

1. **URL encoding** — data: URIs that carry entire pastes, self-viewing HTML, fragment CIDs
2. **QR transport** — single QR for CIDs, multi-QR with Reed-Solomon for payloads
3. **Audio transport** — morse, FSK modem, DTMF tones over speaker/mic
4. **WASM static pastebin** — browser-only, IndexedDB as flatfs, service worker offline
5. **Visual transport** — stego in PNGs, screen capture, video frames
6. **Network composition** — DAG of CIDs = programs, root CID = entire system, self-replication

The eRDFa crate already has most of the building blocks: `stego.rs` (16 encoding strategies), `crypto.rs` (Reed-Solomon), `shards.rs` (k-of-n with Gandalf prime 71), and `erdfa-wasm` (browser runtime).

Where do you want to start? Phase 1 (URL encoding) is the natural first step — a paste that encodes itself as a self-contained `data:` URI you can paste anywhere.

- Output: text, data: URL, QR display, audio (speaker), stego image, morse

ToolUse.content: First, update Cargo.toml with the deps we need:

args.file_text:
[package]
name = "erdfa-wasm"
version = "0.2.0"
edition = "2021"

[lib]
crate-type = ["cdylib"]

[dependencies]
wasm-bindgen = "0.2"
serde = { version = "1.0", features = ["derive"] }
serde_json = "1.0"
sha2 = { version = "0.10", default-features = false }
base64 = { version = "0.22", default-features = false, features = ["alloc"] }
hex = { version = "0.4", default-features = false, features = ["alloc"] }
web-sys = { version = "0.3", features = [
  "Window", "Storage", "Document", "HtmlCanvasElement",
  "CanvasRenderingContext2d", "ImageData",
  "AudioContext", "OscillatorNode", "OscillatorType", "GainNode",
  "AudioDestinationNode",
] }
js-sys = "0.3"

[profile.release]
opt-level = "z"
lto = true
codegen-units = 1
panic = "abort"
strip = true
args.summary: Update erdfa-wasm Cargo.toml with web-sys, sha2, base64, audio deps

Response.content:
Now I see the full picture. You've got:
- `zx81_bbs_voa_old.rs` — Rust BBS with Monster VOA, Leech lattice, Virasoro operators
- `zx81_bbs_door.rs` — Terminal BBS door game with Fractran, Hecke, Galois monitor
- `zx81-bbs.js` — ZX81 terminal emulator rendering on canvas
- `zx81_bbs_optimal_encoding.mzn` — MiniZinc optimal encoding constraints

The eRDFa WASM pad we just wrote has the DTMF/morse/stego encoding. Now we can connect them — the BBS emulator uses real audio tones to transmit data between devices. The ZX81 terminal becomes the UI, and the WASM pad becomes the modem.

The key pieces that make this real:
- `play_bbs()` already generates DTMF tones from data
- `play_morse()` already generates morse audio
- The ZX81 terminal renders on canvas (same as stego encode target)
- The VOA state maps content to Monster group coordinates (same as DASL)

Want me to wire the ZX81 BBS terminal into the eRDFa pad as the UI, so it's a real BBS that communicates via sound? Or build the wasm-pack first and get the current pad running?

Prompt.prompt: we want to support communication over : cassette tapes stored as urls or qr codes or wave files we can paste

args.thought:
The user wants cassette tape-style encoding — like the ZX81/ZX Spectrum/C64 used to load programs from tape. This is Kansas City Standard (KCS) or similar FSK modulation:
- Two frequencies: e.g. 1200Hz = 0, 2400Hz = 1
- Data encoded as audio waveform
- Can be stored as: WAV file, data: URL containing WAV, QR code containing the encoded data

The flow:
1. Data → FSK audio (like a tape save) → WAV bytes
2. WAV bytes → base64 → data: URL (pasteable)
3. WAV bytes → base64 → QR payload
4. Reverse: WAV/URL/QR → FSK decode → data

I need to add to the erdfa-wasm:
- Kansas City Standard FSK encoder (data → WAV PCM samples)
- FSK decoder (WAV PCM samples → data)
- WAV file format writer (PCM samples → .wav bytes)
- WAV file format reader (.wav bytes → PCM samples)

Keep it minimal. The ZX81 used 300 baud with specific tone patterns. Let me implement a simple FSK modem.

content.Text: Tool use was cancelled by the user

args.thought:
The user wants a numbers station — like Cold War shortwave radio broadcasts that read sequences of numbers. With just 10 digit samples (0-9) plus maybe "group" and silence, you can encode any data as spoken numbers. The receiver writes them down and decodes.

The flow:
1. Data → hex string → groups of 5 digits
2. Each digit → PCM audio sample for that digit
3. Concatenate with pauses between groups
4. Output as WAV

For the voice samples, we can either:
- Generate them synthetically using simple waveforms (formant synthesis)
- Use the Web Speech API (SpeechSynthesis) in the browser
- Store tiny pre-recorded samples (but that's heavy for WASM)

Simplest: use Web Speech API from JS side, or generate simple formant-based digit sounds in Rust.

Actually for WASM, the simplest approach is to generate distinguishable tones for each digit (like DTMF but with a spoken-number feel), OR use the browser's SpeechSynthesis API from JS.

Let me add both:
1. Rust side: encode data as digit groups, generate tone-coded audio
2. JS/HTML side: use SpeechSynthesis to actually speak the numbers

Plus the tape/KCS FSK encoder. Let me add all of this to lib.rs in one shot.

Response.content:
Each recording now has three ways to share:

- **⬇ Download WAV** — save to disk, put on USB, email, whatever
- **📋 Share to Pastebin** — uploads the WAV to kant-pastebin as a file (uses the `/upload` endpoint)
- **▶ Play** — play through speakers for another device to capture via mic

The WAV files are self-contained — tape FSK can be decoded back to the original data, numbers station tones can be decoded back too. Full round-trip: type text → encode → download WAV → give to someone → they load WAV → decode → original text.

Since we're in WASM/browser, we can't write to ~/.ipfs/blocks directly. But we can:
- Store blocks in localStorage/IndexedDB keyed by CID
- POST blocks to the pastebin's IPFS endpoint (which writes to flatfs)
- Export everything as a downloadable archive

2. Each encode operation also stores the output as an IPFS block

For zip in WASM without extra deps, I can use a minimal zip writer (just store, no compression needed). Or I can do tar which is simpler (just headers + data).

- Add `ipfs_put(data) -> cid` and `ipfs_get(cid) -> data` to the Pad
- Each encode button also calls ipfs_put on the output

145KB WASM — a full local IPFS client with:

- Every encode (morse, tape WAV, numbers station WAV, stego PNG) auto-stores to local IPFS with CID
- `ipfs_put` / `ipfs_get` / `ipfs_list` — localStorage-backed block store

- **🔄 Sync to Pastebin** — pushes all blocks to kant-pastebin's `/upload` endpoint (which writes to go-ipfs flatfs)
- RDFa triple store with N-Triples import/export

Live at `https://solana.solfunmeme.com/erdfa/`

The full cycle: type text → encode as tape WAV → CID stored → download tar → give to someone → they import → verify CIDs match → knowledge transferred.

args.thought:
The speech capture via MediaRecorder on a MediaStreamDestination won't work because SpeechSynthesis doesn't route through the AudioContext — it goes directly to the system audio output. MediaRecorder can only capture what goes through the AudioContext's destination.

The simplest approach that actually works: use the tone-based numbers station WAV (which we already generate in Rust) as the "saveable" version. The speech is just a convenience for human listening.

But the user wants the actual speech saved. The only reliable way in a browser is to capture system audio, which requires `getDisplayMedia` with audio, or to use a different approach entirely.

Alternative: Instead of trying to capture SpeechSynthesis output (which is a known limitation), I should:
1. Generate the speech text
2. Use the tone-based WAV as the saveable/IPFS version (already works)
3. For actual speech recording, use the microphone to capture the speaker output (loopback) — but that's unreliable

Actually the simplest fix: just save the tone-based numbers station WAV when they click "Save speech". The tones ARE the data. The speech is just a human-friendly overlay. Let me make this clear in the UI and just use the existing `to_numbers_wav` for the save.

Or better: generate the speech, and tell the user to use the ⬇ Download WAV button for the tone version. The speech is ephemeral.

Let me just fix it to save the numbers station tones WAV when clicking save speech, since that's the decodable version anyway.

ToolUse.content: SpeechSynthesis doesn't route through AudioContext — can't capture it with MediaRecorder. The fix: generate the numbers station tone WAV alongside the speech, save that (it's decodable too):

Response.content:
Fixed. Now when you click **🗣 Speak numbers** it:
1. Speaks the numbers aloud via SpeechSynthesis (for humans)
2. Simultaneously generates the tone WAV in Rust (decodable)
3. Stores the tone WAV to local IPFS with CID
4. **⬇ Save speech** downloads the tone WAV

The tone WAV is the machine-readable version — it round-trips perfectly through `from_numbers_wav()`. Browser SpeechSynthesis can't be captured to a file (it bypasses AudioContext entirely).

   - Title

   - Keywords
   - Timestamp
   - IPFS CID (if available)
